aliases: Abalone Mushroom in Butter Cream Sauce

english: Abalone Mushroom in Butter Cream Sauce

food_subgroup: Cooked vegetable and mushroom dish with butter and cream sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for abalone mushroom prepared with butter and cream. FOODON_03315873 (mushroom food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked mushrooms as a category. No specific code for abalone mushroom in butter cream sauce exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for abalone mushroom dish prepared with butter and cream sauce.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of creamy butter abalone mushrooms is approximately 180 g, presented as a side dish or shared plate alongside rice or other mains.

nutrient_source.title: Mushrooms, cooked, stir-fried with butter and cream — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Oyster mushrooms (FDC ID 169403), Butter (FDC ID 173410), Heavy cream (FDC ID 170859), combined at representative proportions (65% mushroom, 20% butter, 15% cream)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for creamy butter abalone mushrooms exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for oyster mushrooms (FDC 169403), unsalted butter (FDC 173410), and heavy whipping cream (FDC 170859) at proportions representative of a typical restaurant preparation (approximately 65% mushroom, 20% butter, 15% cream by cooked weight). Abalone mushrooms (Pleurotus abalonus) are nutritionally very similar to oyster mushrooms (Pleurotus ostreatus) and are treated as equivalent for nutrient estimation purposes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mushrooms and low-carbohydrate cooked vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on oyster mushrooms and butter-cream preparations; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for creamy butter abalone mushrooms. GI estimated at 15 based on the low glycemic character of cooked mushrooms (GI approximately 10–20 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for mushroom-based dishes) combined with the negligible glycemic contribution of butter and cream. Carbohydrate content is low (4.5 g per 100 g), yielding a glycemic load per 180 g serving of approximately 1.2, which is very low. Analogous reference: Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 180 g serving provides approximately 378 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce or low-sodium seasoning and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; however, the high fat content from butter and cream should be considered in the context of overall caloric management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from butter and cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 180 g serving delivers approximately 14.6 g saturated fat, which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily limit of 20 g for cardiovascular risk reduction; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, or request preparation with reduced butter.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Creamy Butter Abalone Mushrooms is a Chinese-style cooked mushroom dish popular in Singapore seafood restaurants and zi char establishments. The dish features abalone mushrooms (Pleurotus abalonus), a variety closely related to oyster mushrooms, prized for their firm, meaty texture and mild, slightly briny flavour reminiscent of abalone seafood. The mushrooms are typically stir-fried or sautéed in generous amounts of butter and finished with a cream-based sauce, often seasoned with garlic, oyster sauce, white pepper, and salt. The result is a rich, savoury, and umami-forward dish with a silky sauce that coats each mushroom cap. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively high in fat (approximately 13 g per 100 g) due to the butter and cream, with saturated fat being the primary clinical concern at around 8 g per 100 g. Carbohydrate content is low at approximately 4.5 g per 100 g, giving the dish a very low glycemic index of approximately 15 and a negligible glycemic load per serving. Protein content is modest at approximately 2.8 g per 100 g. Sodium is moderate at approximately 210 mg per 100 g, largely from oyster sauce and seasoning. The dish is suitable for vegetarians (when oyster sauce is replaced with vegetarian alternatives) and is generally halal. It is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy allergies. Clinically, the dish is low-risk for blood glucose management but warrants caution for individuals managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension due to its saturated fat and sodium content. It is commonly served as a shared side dish alongside steamed rice in a Chinese seafood or zi char meal context in Singapore.
common_user_queries: What are creamy butter abalone mushrooms?
Is creamy butter mushroom dish suitable for diabetics?
What is abalone mushroom in Chinese cooking?
Can I eat butter mushrooms if I have high blood pressure?
What does abalone mushroom taste like?
meal_context: shared side dish in Chinese seafood restaurant

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for creamy butter abalone mushrooms exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central component data for oyster mushrooms, butter, and cream at representative proportions. Abalone mushrooms are nutritionally analogous to oyster mushrooms. GI is estimated from analogous mushroom dish data in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (oyster mushrooms FDC 169403, unsalted butter FDC 173410, heavy whipping cream FDC 170859) at proportions of approximately 65% mushroom, 20% butter, 15% cream by cooked weight. Actual restaurant preparation may vary. 2. Abalone mushrooms (Pleurotus abalonus) are treated as nutritionally equivalent to oyster mushrooms (Pleurotus ostreatus) for estimation purposes, consistent with published mycological literature. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked mushroom GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 4. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients at the level of precision required for this estimation. 5. Sodium estimate of 210 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant seasoning with oyster sauce; home preparations may differ significantly. 6. The dish is listed under cuisine tags including Crab and Lobster in the source field survey data; these tags appear to reflect the restaurant category (Riverfront Seafood) rather than the dish ingredients and are not carried forward. 7. is_vegetarian is set to true as the dish contains no meat or seafood; however, oyster sauce (derived from oysters) is commonly used, making strict vegetarian status dependent on preparation. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.3 g per 100 g reflects naturally occurring trans fats from dairy (butter) as reported in USDA data; this is not industrially produced trans fat.
